docs.text: About This Site This portfolio is built with MkDocs and hosted on GitHub Pages . The source files are version-controlled in a public GitHub repository. Toolchain Tool Purpose MkDocs Static site generator; converts Markdown files to HTML GitHub Version control and remote repository hosting GitHub Pages Free static site hosting, deployed via GitHub Actions Visual Studio Code Local file editing Terminal Running MkDocs commands and Git operations Workflow Edit or create .md files locally in VS Code Preview changes locally with mkdocs serve Stage and commit changes via Git Push to main — GitHub Actions deploys automatically Deployment Initially deployed using mkdocs gh-deploy from the command line. Migrated to a GitHub Actions workflow to automate deployment on every push to main , eliminating the manual deploy step. The Actions workflow builds the site and publishes to GitHub Pages automatically whenever changes are merged. Source: .github/workflows/ Repository Source code: github.com/nellcgram/nellcgram.github.io

docs.text: How to Read Your Explanation of Benefits (EOB) What Is an EOB? An Explanation of Benefits (EOB) is a document your health insurance company sends after you receive medical care. It is not a bill. It shows what your doctor charged, what your insurance paid, and what you may owe. What This Guide Covers This guide explains each section of your EOB so you know exactly what you are looking at. Your Personal Information At the top of your EOB you will find your name, address, member ID, and insurance group number. Check that this information is correct. The Four Key Numbers Every EOB shows four numbers that explain how your medical costs are shared between you and your insurance company. Billed Amount The total your doctor or provider charged for the service. Allowed Amount The amount your insurance company agrees to pay for the service based on your plan. This is usually less than the billed amount. Insurance Payment The portion of the allowed amount that your insurance paid directly to your provider. Amount You Owe What you must pay after your insurance pays its share. This may include your deductible, copay, or coinsurance. In-Network and Out-of-Network Costs Your costs depend on whether your provider is in your insurance network. In-network providers have agreements with your insurance company. Find the "network savings" box on your EOB to see how much you saved by using an in-network provider. Out-of-network providers do not have agreements with your insurance company. You may owe more than expected. Check the network savings line. If it shows $0, you used an out-of-network provider. Remark Codes Remark codes are short notes from your insurance company. They appear as 2 or 3 letters or numbers and explain details about your costs, such as why a service was partially paid or denied. Find the description of each code at the bottom of your EOB. How to Check for Errors Errors on EOBs are common. Checking yours can save you money. Check the date and service. Did you receive this care on this date? Compare your EOB to your doctor's bill. The amount you owe should match. Check your network status. Did you see an in-network doctor? Your network savings should not show $0. Look up any remark codes. The descriptions are at the bottom of your EOB. If something looks wrong, call your doctor's billing office first. Then call your insurance company if needed.
docs.title: EOB Guide
How to Read Your Explanation of Benefits (EOB)

docs.text: Help Center Article How to use Story Board view in Scribbler View sample About This sample Help Center article documents a fictional writing application called Scribbler, following documentation patterns used by Google Play. Why I chose this project Rather than document an existing application like Scrivener, the author created a fictionalized interface with similar terminology. The scope focuses specifically on the Story Board view for project outlining rather than comprehensive application coverage. The article emphasizes customization features such as color coding and metadata creation to showcase information organization and discoverability. Content strategy The article includes information architecture annotations demonstrating a content model designed for single-source publishing within a Content Management System. The organizational structure follows a Help Center "How to / About" framework that begins with benefits, progresses through tasks and tips, and ends with related resources. Content moves from fundamental to advanced customization options. Writing and design approach Style standards (based on Google's style guide): Tone: friendly, empathetic, conversational Clarity: 7th grade reading level, active voice, front-loaded sentences Formatting: Sentence case for titles, title case for UI, breadcrumb navigation Design decisions: The author differentiated HTML tag usage for both visual hierarchy and accessibility: kbd tags for keyboard shortcuts, code tags for UI elements, and strong tags for conceptual emphasis. Two callout box styles were implemented — blue for informational context and yellow for warnings.
docs.title: Help Center Article

docs.text: Internal Knowledge Base Article Agent guide to processing loan extensions View sample Project overview This sample knowledge base article was created for library circulation staff, based on publicly available Los Angeles Public Library policies. It showcases internal documentation designed to help customer service representatives handle patron inquiries. Content strategy Organization: The article follows agent workflows, moving from frequent tasks like renewals to less common situations such as lost cards or damaged materials, enabling quick reference. Scripted responses: The article includes templated answers and prepared responses that staff can use as starting points when communicating with patrons, similar to resources created for Google support teams. Writing and design approach Visual hierarchy for reference: HTML code tags for interface elements Collapsible sections to reduce cognitive load Color-coded reference boxes emphasizing important details Style standards (based on Google's style guide): Tone: conversational, empathetic, welcoming Clarity: accessible reading level, active voice, information prioritized by importance Formatting: sentence case headers, proper capitalization for UI elements, navigation breadcrumbs Responses center customer empathy Key features Task-based workflows for typical circulation scenarios Eligibility reference table for quick policy verification Template email and chat responses with customizable placeholders Metrics reporting guidance for escalation decisions
docs.title: KB Article

docs.text: Release Notes eReader App Monthly Updates View sample About Sample release notes were created for a fictional eReader application, drawing inspiration from Google Play's release note style. Reference: Release notes for Google Play Games on PC My approach Rather than presenting technical details, these notes spotlight practical user advantages. The strategy reduces support inquiries while promoting feature adoption. Key elements User value first: Focus on what users gain from updates — for instance, "Adds a streak tracker that shows how many days you've read in a row," instead of purely technical descriptions Action-oriented language: Descriptions enable users to understand how to leverage updated features Balanced scope: Mix of major features with performance improvements to establish realistic user expectations
docs.title: Release Notes

docs.text: View sample
docs.title: eReader App Monthly Updates
